items.myth: Vectors are only arrows in 2D or 3D space.
items.reality: Vectors can be any objects that support vector addition and scalar multiplication satisfying the vector space axioms, including high-dimensional feature vectors, functions and embeddings.
items.example: A 1536-dimensional text embedding from a modern encoder is just as much a vector as a 3D arrow; the same rules for span, basis and subspaces apply.

items.myth: Any set of vectors that spans a space is automatically a basis.
items.reality: A basis must both span the space and be linearly independent; spanning alone allows redundant vectors and non-unique coordinate representations.
items.example: In R^2, the vectors `(1, 0)`, `(0, 1)` and `(1, 1)` span the space, but only the first two form a basis because the third is a sum of the first two.

items.myth: Subspaces must always have lower dimension than the ambient space.
items.reality: A subspace is allowed to equal the whole space; the ambient vector space itself is always a subspace of itself.
items.example: R^3 has many proper subspaces (lines and planes through the origin), but R^3 itself is also a subspace according to the formal definition.
